1.title: Welcome to Science & Tech of Space / Defining Science

questions.prompt: What is a hypothesis?
choices: A confirmed generalized pattern observed in nature
A testable but not-yet-confirmed proposed explanation
A mathematical equation describing planetary motion
A rigorously confirmed explanation for a phenomenon
questions.explanation: A hypothesis is a tentative, testable proposal. Once rigorously confirmed it becomes a theory; a confirmed pattern without explanation is a law.

questions.prompt: What best describes a scientific theory?
choices: An opinion held by a majority of scientists
A confirmed explanation for WHY something occurs
An untested guess about how something works
A description of a pattern without an explanation
questions.explanation: A theory explains the underlying mechanism for a phenomenon and has survived repeated testing. It is not 'just a guess' in the colloquial sense.

questions.prompt: A scientific LAW does which of the following?
choices: Explains WHY a natural phenomenon occurs
Describes a generalized pattern without explaining it
Proposes an untested guess about a phenomenon
States a belief accepted without any evidence
questions.explanation: A law states a reliable pattern (e.g., F=ma) but does not explain why it holds. Theories explain; laws describe.

questions.prompt: Which learning outcome is emphasized in 'Scientific Literacy' for this course?
choices: Interpreting data and drawing appropriate conclusions
Writing software to simulate rocket trajectories
Designing and building a physical telescope
Memorizing every constellation in the sky
questions.explanation: Scientific literacy is about reading data critically and drawing conclusions, not about specialized technical skills like coding or hardware build.

questions.prompt: What is the primary focus of NST1010?
choices: History of warfare and military technology
Space science, origins, and life beyond Earth
questions.explanation: NST1010 covers the science and tech of space, cosmic origins, and the search for life beyond Earth.

questions.prompt: Which is NOT an ideal property of a scientific discipline?
questions.explanation: Science strives to be objective, empirical, and repeatable. Subjectivity is the opposite of what makes science trustworthy.

questions.prompt: 'Underdetermination' in science means:
choices: The hypothesis was too narrowly defined
Funding is never sufficient for the study
Data supports more than one explanation
Data has been fabricated by the researcher
questions.explanation: Underdetermination is when the available data fits multiple competing hypotheses; more or different data is needed to distinguish them.

questions.prompt: A controlled experiment requires:
choices: A single group with many variables changing
Control and experimental groups, one variable
A theoretical model with no measurements taken
questions.explanation: Controlled experiments isolate cause by changing one variable at a time and comparing the experimental group to a control group.

questions.prompt: Observational science CANNOT definitively establish:
questions.explanation: Observation reveals patterns and correlations, but causation requires controlled manipulation of variables. Correlation is not causation.

questions.prompt: Karl Popper's test of science is based on:
questions.explanation: Popper argued science advances by disproving claims, not confirming them. A real scientific claim must be falsifiable: testable in a way that could prove it wrong.

questions.prompt: In deductive logic: If A then B. Which is valid?
questions.explanation: This is the contrapositive: if A implies B, then absence of B guarantees absence of A. 'B means A' (affirming the consequent) is a classic fallacy.

questions.prompt: The 1919 eclipse test supported which hypothesis?
choices: Stars are made primarily of iron
The sun emits low-frequency radio waves
Light has no mass and no momentum
questions.explanation: Eddington's 1919 eclipse measured starlight bending around the Sun, confirming Einstein's prediction that mass curves spacetime.

questions.prompt: The null hypothesis (H0) usually states:
choices: No effect; differences are random chance
Only the alternative explanations are valid
The experiment was a methodological failure
questions.explanation: The null hypothesis is the default 'nothing happening' assumption. Statistical tests try to reject it in favor of the alternative.

questions.prompt: In the northern hemisphere, the angle between Polaris and the horizon equals:
choices: The observer's altitude above sea level
questions.explanation: Polaris sits nearly above Earth's rotational axis, so its altitude above the horizon equals your latitude. At the North Pole it's overhead; at the equator it's at the horizon.

questions.prompt: The Earth rotates at approximately:
questions.explanation: 360 degrees divided by 24 hours equals 15 degrees per hour. That is also why each time zone spans roughly 15 degrees of longitude.

questions.prompt: A sidereal day (relative to the stars) is:
questions.explanation: A sidereal day is one full rotation relative to the stars: 23h 56m. The 24-hour solar day is longer because Earth also moves along its orbit, requiring extra rotation to face the Sun again.

questions.prompt: The ecliptic is:
choices: The Milky Way as seen from Earth
The sun's apparent path across the sky
questions.explanation: The ecliptic is the Sun's apparent yearly path, which is also the plane of Earth's orbit. The zodiac constellations lie along it.

questions.prompt: What causes the seasons?
choices: Solar flares and surface activity cycles
Earth's axial tilt and sunlight directness
The Moon's monthly phases and orbit
Earth getting closer to the sun in summer
questions.explanation: Earth's 23.5-degree axial tilt makes sunlight strike each hemisphere more directly in summer and obliquely in winter. Distance from the Sun barely changes.

questions.prompt: The Copernican Principle states:
choices: We are the center of the universe
We are NOT the center of the universe
questions.explanation: Copernicus displaced Earth from the center: we occupy no privileged location in the cosmos. The principle now extends broadly: nothing about our viewpoint is special.

questions.prompt: The Fermi Paradox asks:
choices: Why is the night sky dark and cold?
If intelligent life is common, where is it?
Why do all stars twinkle in the sky?
Why does time only move in one direction?
questions.explanation: Given billions of stars and likely planets, naive math predicts we should see signs of intelligent life. Fermi's question: 'Where is everybody?'

4.title: Forces and Energy of the Universe

questions.prompt: Whose laws describe planetary motion in elliptical orbits?
questions.explanation: Kepler's three laws describe elliptical orbits, equal areas in equal times, and the period-distance relationship. Newton later derived them from gravity.

questions.prompt: The Law of Conservation of Energy states that energy:
choices: Is neither created nor destroyed, only changed
Can be destroyed but cannot be created
Is always lost as heat in any process
Can be created but cannot be destroyed
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved: it transforms between forms (kinetic, potential, thermal, etc.) but the total stays constant in a closed system.

questions.prompt: Which four fundamental forces exist in the universe?
questions.explanation: The four fundamental forces are gravity, electromagnetism, the strong nuclear force, and the weak nuclear force. The other choices list energy types or everyday effects, not fundamental forces.

questions.prompt: Newton's 'Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica' presented:
choices: Laws of motion and universal gravitation
questions.explanation: The Principia (1687) introduced Newton's three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation, founding classical mechanics.

questions.prompt: General Relativity, Quantum Mechanics, and Thermodynamics are examples of:
questions.explanation: These are major, still-active branches of physics. Each handles a different regime (gravity/large scale, atomic/quantum scale, heat and energy flow).

questions.prompt: Wavelength is defined as:
choices: The maximum vertical height of a wave
Distance between successive crests of a wave
The number of complete waves per second
The propagation speed of the wave
questions.explanation: Wavelength is the spatial repeat distance, crest to crest. Wave height is amplitude; cycles per second is frequency.

questions.prompt: Frequency is measured in:
questions.explanation: One hertz equals one cycle per second. Watts measure power, joules energy, m/s speed.

questions.prompt: The speed of electromagnetic waves in a vacuum is approximately:
questions.explanation: The speed of light c is about 3 x 10^8 m/s (more precisely 2.998 x 10^8 m/s). 9.8 m/s^2 is Earth's gravitational acceleration.

questions.prompt: Planck's equation (E = hf) relates:
questions.explanation: Planck's relation says photon energy is proportional to frequency, with Planck's constant h as the link. Higher-frequency light carries more energy per photon.

questions.prompt: In the Bohr model of hydrogen, electrons:
questions.explanation: Bohr's quantized orbits explain why atoms emit and absorb only specific wavelengths: electrons can only occupy discrete energy levels.

questions.prompt: The relationship for electromagnetic waves is:
questions.explanation: For any wave, c = lambda x f. Since c is fixed in vacuum, longer wavelength means lower frequency, and vice versa.

questions.prompt: Emission and absorption spectra tell us about:
choices: The total mass of the object only
questions.explanation: Each element produces a unique spectral fingerprint from its quantized energy-level transitions, letting us identify what stars and gas clouds are made of.

questions.prompt: Which wavelengths track the HOTTEST, highest-energy objects like black hole accretion disks?
questions.explanation: Hotter objects emit at shorter, higher-energy wavelengths. Million-degree plasma around black holes glows in X-ray and gamma-ray; cool dust glows in IR/radio.

questions.prompt: Infrared light is best for observing:
choices: Very hot plasmas and accretion disks
questions.explanation: Cool objects (a few hundred to a few thousand K) and dust-shrouded star nurseries radiate strongly in IR; IR also penetrates dust that blocks visible light.

questions.prompt: What do telescopes primarily do?
choices: Measure object temperature only at distance
Create new light from their own optics
Amplify sound from very distant sources
questions.explanation: A telescope is fundamentally a photon bucket: it collects and focuses light from astronomical sources. Detectors then turn those photons into data.

questions.prompt: A refracting telescope uses:
questions.explanation: Refractors bend light through lenses. Reflectors use mirrors instead, which is why most large modern telescopes are reflectors (lenses get heavy fast).

questions.prompt: Adaptive optics compensates for:
choices: Cosmic ray hits striking the detector
Light pollution from cities and roadways
Cracks or visible defects in the lens
questions.explanation: Adaptive optics flexes a deformable mirror in real time to cancel the wavefront wobble caused by atmospheric turbulence, sharpening ground-based images.

questions.prompt: The JWST primary mirror is:
questions.explanation: JWST's segmented gold-coated primary is 6.5 meters across. Hubble's is 2.4 meters; JWST has roughly seven times Hubble's collecting area.

questions.prompt: LIGO detects:
questions.explanation: LIGO is a 4-km laser interferometer that senses tiny spacetime stretches from gravitational waves (e.g., merging black holes), not electromagnetic radiation.

questions.prompt: Photometry vs spectroscopy: spectroscopy measures:
questions.explanation: Spectroscopy splits light by wavelength to reveal composition, motion, and physical conditions. Photometry just counts total photons (brightness).

questions.prompt: Why must X-ray observatories like Chandra be in space?
questions.explanation: Earth's atmosphere absorbs X-rays (good for life, bad for astronomy). To detect X-rays at all, the telescope must be above the atmosphere.

questions.prompt: Astronomers design telescope systems around four main properties:
questions.explanation: The four design properties trade off: collecting area (faintness), spatial (sharpness), spectral (color/wavelength detail), temporal (time changes).

questions.prompt: A larger collecting area (mirror) primarily helps a telescope:
choices: See fainter objects via more photons
questions.explanation: More mirror area = more photons captured per second, which means fainter sources rise above noise. Resolution depends on aperture too, but sensitivity is the main payoff.

questions.prompt: Spatial resolution refers to:
choices: The number of photons per second
The total mass of the telescope
Ability to separate two close sources
questions.explanation: Spatial resolution is image sharpness: the smallest angular separation at which two sources stay distinct. Photon count is sensitivity, not resolution.

questions.prompt: Earth's atmosphere is largely transparent in two main 'windows':
questions.explanation: Visible and radio pass through cleanly, which is why ground-based observatories thrive there. UV/X-ray/gamma are blocked, forcing space missions for those bands.

questions.prompt: Why can't X-rays be focused by traditional parabolic mirrors at normal incidence?
choices: Mirrors are too small for X-rays
X-rays are too cheap to focus
X-rays travel too slowly to reflect
questions.explanation: X-rays carry too much energy to bounce off a mirror hit head-on; they pass through or get absorbed. Only very shallow grazing angles give reflection.

questions.prompt: X-ray telescopes use grazing incidence optics, meaning:
choices: X-rays skim heavy-metal mirrors at <1 deg
questions.explanation: X-rays graze nested gold or iridium-coated mirrors at angles under 1 degree, like skipping stones. Multiple nested shells boost collecting area.

questions.prompt: Chandra's ACIS instrument is:
choices: A CCD imager logging photon X,Y,t,E
questions.explanation: ACIS records every X-ray photon with position, time, and energy. That photon list lets astronomers build images, spectra, and light curves from a single dataset.

questions.prompt: From a Chandra photon list (X, Y, time, energy), astronomers can directly produce:
questions.explanation: With each photon's position, time, and energy logged, you can map (image), bin by energy (spectrum), or bin by time (light curve).

questions.prompt: HETG and LETG on Chandra are:
questions.explanation: The High and Low Energy Transmission Gratings disperse X-rays by wavelength for high-resolution spectroscopy.

questions.prompt: The Fermi Gamma-Ray Space Telescope detects gamma rays via:
questions.explanation: High-energy gamma rays convert into electron-positron pairs in the detector; tracking those particles reveals the gamma ray's energy and direction.

questions.prompt: Radio telescopes are particularly useful for detecting:
choices: Cold molecules and energetic galaxy processes
Only visible-light photons in the sky
Only stars hotter than the Sun
questions.explanation: Radio reveals cold gas (molecular line emission) and high-energy synchrotron emission from active galactic nuclei and pulsars.

questions.prompt: ALMA (Atacama Large Millimeter Array), located in Chile, primarily observes:
choices: Radio/mm waves; cold gas and dust
questions.explanation: ALMA's millimeter/sub-mm wavelengths are ideal for cold molecular clouds, protoplanetary disks, and dusty galaxies. The high, dry Atacama site keeps water vapor low.

questions.prompt: The diffraction limit of a telescope:
choices: Has been overcome at all wavelengths
Applies only at radio wavelengths now
Limits resolution by aperture and wavelength
questions.explanation: Resolution scales as wavelength divided by aperture. Longer wavelengths or smaller mirrors mean blurrier images; this is fundamental physics, not a fixable flaw.

questions.prompt: Radio interferometry combines signals from many antennas with precise timing to achieve:
choices: Reduced overall sensitivity to faint sources
Resolution equal to a dish of the baseline
Only narrowband observations and nothing more
Lower spatial resolution than a single dish
questions.explanation: Combining antennas separated by distance B gives the resolution of a dish of diameter B. Sensitivity, however, only equals the sum of the actual antenna areas.

questions.prompt: The Murchison Widefield Array (MWA) in Australia points electronically because:
choices: It uses extremely fast motors to point
Software is faster than mechanical pointing
No moving parts; software phases signals
It is a visible-light optical telescope
questions.explanation: MWA dipoles stay fixed; the array steers by digitally adjusting the phase of each antenna's signal. No motors needed.

questions.prompt: LIGO is fundamentally:
questions.explanation: LIGO uses two 4-km perpendicular laser arms; gravitational waves stretch spacetime and alter the path lengths by sub-proton amounts.

questions.prompt: Active optics on survey telescopes (like Vera C. Rubin Observatory) are designed to:
choices: Eliminate the need for any filters
Boost the apparent brightness of a star
questions.explanation: Active optics slowly correct the mirror's shape against gravity, thermal flexing, and wind. Adaptive optics is the faster, atmosphere-correcting cousin.

questions.prompt: The Vera C. Rubin Observatory's primary mirror is approximately:
questions.explanation: Rubin's primary is 8.4 m; combined with its huge field of view, it images the entire southern sky every few nights for the LSST survey.

questions.prompt: Why is no single telescope sufficient to study all astrophysical phenomena?
choices: All telescopes effectively see the same thing
Each EM band needs matched optics and detectors
Cost reasons alone drive the need for many
Telescopes break easily and need redundancy
questions.explanation: Different wavelengths require fundamentally different optics (lenses, mirrors, grazing incidence, dipoles) and detectors. No one design works across the whole spectrum.

questions.prompt: You are designing a space mission to study the hottest, highest-energy phenomena in the Universe (e.g., black hole accretion, supernova remnants). (a) Which part of the EM spectrum should you target, and why? (b) What kind of optics must your telescope use, and why are traditional parabolic mirrors not suitable? (c) Name an operating space mission that already does this kind of science.
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) X-rays (and gamma rays) — hottest, highest-energy processes radiate strongly there; Earth's atmosphere blocks X-rays so the mission must be in space. (b) Grazing-incidence optics with low (<1 degree) reflection angles, often coated with gold or iridium; X-rays are ionizing and don't reflect efficiently at normal incidence on parabolic mirrors. (c) Chandra X-Ray Observatory (also acceptable: XMM-Newton, NuSTAR, IXPE for X-ray; Fermi for gamma-ray).

questions.prompt: Compare the four telescope design properties (collecting area, spatial resolution, spectral resolution, temporal resolution). (a) Define each. (b) Which property matters most for detecting transiting exoplanets, and why? (c) Which property matters most for spectroscopically identifying the composition of a distant galaxy?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Collecting area — mirror size, governs faintness limit; spatial resolution — sharpness, ability to separate close sources; spectral resolution — ability to resolve photon energies/wavelengths into fine bins; temporal resolution — ability to detect changes in brightness over time. (b) Transits: temporal resolution (catching periodic dimming) plus collecting area (high signal-to-noise on a single transit) — Kepler/TESS prioritized both. (c) Distant galaxy composition: spectral resolution (to identify individual emission/absorption lines and infer composition/redshift), with collecting area to gather enough light from a faint source.

questions.prompt: Radio interferometry is a workaround for the diffraction limit. (a) State the diffraction-limit relationship qualitatively. (b) Explain how an array of antennas like the VLA or MWA can match the resolution of an impossibly large single dish. (c) Why does this trick not directly give you more raw photons / collecting area?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Resolution (smallest resolvable separation) is proportional to wavelength / aperture diameter — so longer wavelength or smaller aperture means worse resolution. (b) Combining signals from many antennas with precise timing produces interference patterns; the maximum baseline between antennas plays the role of an effective aperture, achieving spatial resolution as if the array were one giant dish. (c) The collecting area equals the SUM of individual antenna areas, not the area of the synthesized 'big dish' — so interferometry gains resolution but not raw sensitivity beyond the actual antenna count.

8.title: Planet Formation Pt 1: Habitable Zones & Star Types

questions.prompt: The Habitable Zone is defined as:
choices: Specifically the orbit of Earth around the Sun
The plane of the Milky Way galaxy
The region within 1 AU of every star
Orbital range allowing liquid surface water
questions.explanation: The HZ is the orbital band where liquid surface water can persist: not too hot, not too cold. Its location depends on the star, not on a fixed distance like 1 AU.

questions.prompt: The Habitable Zone is also nicknamed:
questions.explanation: 'Goldilocks Zone' captures the just-right temperature window. The frost line is much farther out (where ices condense); the Roche limit is a tidal-disruption boundary.

questions.prompt: The location of the Habitable Zone around a given star depends on:
choices: The star's size and luminosity output
The current age of the universe
questions.explanation: More luminous stars push the liquid-water zone outward; dimmer stars pull it inward. Color (temperature) correlates with luminosity but luminosity sets the actual distance.

questions.prompt: Our Sun is classified as a:
questions.explanation: The Sun is a G2V main-sequence star. M-dwarfs are smaller and cooler; neutron stars and white dwarfs are stellar remnants.

questions.prompt: Red dwarf stars (M-dwarfs):
choices: Are always larger and hotter than the Sun
Cannot host any planets in their orbit
Are the rarest type in the Milky Way
Are the most common type (~75% of stars)
questions.explanation: M-dwarfs make up roughly 3 out of every 4 stars in the galaxy. They're small, cool, and very long-lived, but most are too dim to see without a telescope.

questions.prompt: Planets in a red dwarf's tight habitable zone face what major challenge?
choices: Extreme X-ray/UV flares from the star
They are too cold for any chemistry
They never form any atmosphere at all
Their orbits sit too far from the star
questions.explanation: M-dwarfs flare violently in X-ray/UV, and their HZ sits very close in. Atmospheres of close-in planets get stripped, threatening habitability.

questions.prompt: The TRAPPIST-1 system is notable because:
choices: It contains only a single Jupiter-like planet
It contains no detectable planets at all
Seven Earth-sized rocky worlds around an M-dwarf
It is the closest star system to our Sun
questions.explanation: TRAPPIST-1 is a unique 'mini solar system' of seven Earth-sized rocky worlds, several near the HZ, all transiting and accessible to JWST.

questions.prompt: According to preliminary JWST observations of TRAPPIST-1 d:
choices: It has a dense global liquid ocean
It has been confirmed as fully habitable
It appears to have NO detectable atmosphere
It has an Earth-like nitrogen atmosphere
questions.explanation: Early JWST data on TRAPPIST-1 d shows no detectable atmosphere, suggesting M-dwarf flares may strip atmospheres off close-in HZ planets.

questions.prompt: K-dwarfs (orange dwarfs) are sometimes called 'true Goldilocks stars' because they:
choices: Are identical in every way to the Sun
Cannot host planets in their orbits
Burn out in only a few million years
Burn long and steady; ~3x more common than G
questions.explanation: K-dwarfs combine long lifetimes (tens of Gyr), greater abundance than G-stars, and milder flare activity than M-dwarfs: a sweet spot for stable habitability.

questions.prompt: Earth-sized planets are most often detected around red dwarfs because:
choices: Transits block more of the small star's light
Red dwarfs always host Earth-sized planets
Larger stars are too noisy to observe well
Astronomers do not look at large stars
questions.explanation: A small planet creates a much bigger fractional dip across a small star, making transits easier to detect. Same planet around the Sun would hide in the noise.

questions.prompt: NASA's Target Star Catalog supports the future Habitable Worlds Observatory by:
choices: Cataloging nearby stars for biosignature search
Tracking the position of every spacecraft
Archiving the schematics of major telescopes
Listing every confirmed exoplanet ever found
questions.explanation: The catalog lists priority nearby stars where HWO will hunt for Earth-like planets and atmospheric biosignatures.

questions.prompt: Compared to G-stars like the Sun, K-dwarfs have a main-sequence lifetime that is:
choices: About the same as the Sun's lifetime
Much shorter than the Sun's lifetime
Effectively zero years on main sequence
Much longer; tens of billions of years
questions.explanation: K-dwarfs burn fuel slower than the Sun, lasting tens of billions of years: more time for life to emerge and evolve.

questions.prompt: Why does the Habitable Zone of a hotter star sit farther from the star than for a cooler star?
choices: More energy pushes liquid-water orbit outward
Cooler stars have no habitable zone at all
Hotter stars are smaller in radius
Hotter stars host fewer orbiting planets
questions.explanation: Hotter, more luminous stars deliver more energy at any given distance, so the liquid-water orbit shifts outward to where the flux is right.

questions.prompt: The 'searching where the light is better' rationale for the Habitable Zone refers to:
choices: Earth being the center of the universe
Liquid-water life is easier to look for
The Sun being the only star with life
questions.explanation: We focus on Earth-like, water-based life because we know what to look for. Other chemistries may exist but we lack the tools to detect them.

questions.prompt: Large gaseous worlds like Jupiter are considered LESS likely to host life mainly because:
choices: They emit too much radio noise to study
No solid surface; crushing atmospheric pressure
They rotate too fast for stable conditions
They orbit too far from any host star
questions.explanation: Gas giants lack a surface for chemistry to settle on, and pressures deep inside crush molecules. Their moons are often better life candidates.

questions.prompt: Compare G-, K-, and M-type stars as candidates for hosting habitable planets. (a) Briefly describe each star type. (b) Discuss the tradeoffs each presents for habitability — lifetime, abundance, flare activity, habitable zone width. (c) Which would you prioritize in a search for Earth-like worlds, and why?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) G — Sun-like, yellow, ~10 Gyr lifetime, moderate abundance. K — orange dwarfs, cooler/dimmer than Sun, tens of Gyr lifetimes, ~3x more abundant than G. M — red dwarfs, smallest/coolest/dimmest, most abundant (~75%), very long lifetimes, prone to violent flares with high X-ray/UV. (b) G: well-studied, life arose around the Sun, but rarer and shorter-lived. K: best-of-both — long-lived, common, more stable than M. M: tightest HZ (close-in tidal locking risk + flare sterilization risk) but vastly more numerous. (c) Strong answer prioritizes K-dwarfs ('Goldilocks stars') for stability, lifetime, and abundance — though M-dwarfs are tempting for sheer numbers and easier transit detection. Either choice acceptable if justified.

questions.prompt: TRAPPIST-1 is a small M-dwarf system with seven Earth-sized worlds. (a) Why has it been a focal point for exoplanet research? (b) What did preliminary JWST observations reveal about TRAPPIST-1 d? (c) What does this finding imply for the habitability of M-dwarf systems generally?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Seven Earth-sized rocky worlds, several in the M-dwarf's habitable zone, all transiting and accessible to JWST atmosphere studies — a unique 'mini solar system' for testing habitability around the most common stellar type. (b) JWST's preliminary results suggest TRAPPIST-1 d has no detectable atmosphere. (c) M-dwarfs may strip the atmospheres off close-in habitable-zone planets via XUV flares and stellar wind, casting doubt on the habitability of Earth-sized worlds in tight M-dwarf habitable zones — but research continues on TRAPPIST-1's outer planets, which may retain atmospheres at greater distances from the star.

questions.prompt: A nebula is:
choices: A cloud of gas and dust
questions.explanation: Nebulae are interstellar clouds of gas and dust. They are the raw material for new stars and planetary systems.

questions.prompt: The OBAFGKM spectral classification goes from:
questions.explanation: OBAFGKM runs hottest (O, ~30,000+ K, blue) to coolest (M, ~3,000 K, red). 'Oh Be A Fine Guy/Girl, Kiss Me' is the classic mnemonic.

questions.prompt: Hydrostatic equilibrium in the sun means:
choices: All of its hydrogen fuel has burned out
The sun is cooling down rapidly now
The sun does not rotate at all
questions.explanation: Fusion-driven gas pressure pushes outward; gravity pulls inward. The balance keeps the Sun stable in size for billions of years.

questions.prompt: The 'frost line' in a solar system is where:
choices: Water freezes so ices become solid
questions.explanation: Beyond the frost line, water and other volatiles freeze into solid ices, providing extra solid material for planet cores: the reason gas giants form out there.

questions.prompt: Outer planets became gas giants mainly because:
choices: They are older than the inner planets
Cores beyond frost line captured more gas
They orbited the sun faster than rocky worlds
They collided heavily with passing asteroids
questions.explanation: Past the frost line, ices made bigger solid cores quickly. Those cores became massive enough to gravitationally swallow huge gas envelopes.

questions.prompt: Conservation of angular momentum explains why protostars:
choices: Form a rotating disk of gas around them
questions.explanation: As a slowly spinning cloud collapses, it spins faster, just like a skater pulling arms in. The result is a flattened, rotating protoplanetary disk.

questions.prompt: The Kepler mission primarily detects exoplanets via:
questions.explanation: Kepler watched ~150,000 stars for periodic dips in brightness as planets crossed in front, the transit method.

questions.prompt: The radial velocity method detects exoplanets by:
choices: Measuring Doppler shifts of the host star
questions.explanation: A planet tugs its host star, producing tiny Doppler shifts in the star's spectrum. Bigger or closer planets produce bigger wobbles.

questions.prompt: Current estimates of star formation rate in the Milky Way:
choices: About one million stars per year
questions.explanation: The Milky Way produces roughly 5-10 solar masses worth of new stars per year, modest but steady.

questions.prompt: Escape velocity depends on:
choices: Mass and radius of the body
The local time of the day
The age of the escaping object
questions.explanation: Escape velocity v = sqrt(2GM/r): more mass or smaller radius means a steeper gravity well to climb out of.

10.title: Origin of Life: Early Earth and Asteroids

questions.prompt: The Heavy Bombardment Period is evidenced by:
choices: The estimated age of Earth's oceans
Orbital paths of the outer giant planets
Strength of Earth's global magnetic field
Heavy cratering on the Moon and Mercury
questions.explanation: The Moon and Mercury preserve the cratering record because they lack erosion and plate tectonics. Earth was hit just as hard but reshaped its surface.

questions.prompt: OSIRIS-REx returned samples from which asteroid in 2023?
questions.explanation: OSIRIS-REx grabbed a sample from carbonaceous asteroid Bennu and dropped it back to Earth in September 2023.

questions.prompt: Samples from Bennu contained which of the following?
choices: Only iron, nickel, and trace metals
Pure water ice and nothing organic
No detectable organic compounds at all
questions.explanation: Bennu yielded 14 of the 20 amino acids life uses plus all 5 DNA/RNA nucleobases, hard evidence that life's building blocks formed in space.

questions.prompt: Which asteroid/meteorite type contains the most organic material?
questions.explanation: Carbonaceous chondrites are carbon-rich and preserve early-solar-system organics. Iron and stony-iron types come from differentiated cores or mantles, not organic-rich layers.

questions.prompt: In Bennu samples, ammonia + formaldehyde are significant because they:
choices: Indicate liquid water inside Bennu today
React to build amino acids in conditions
questions.explanation: Ammonia plus formaldehyde under the right conditions yields amino acids (Strecker synthesis). Their presence shows the chemistry leading to biology was already running in space.

questions.prompt: What is the 'exogenous' hypothesis for the origin of life's building blocks?
choices: They formed deep in the Earth's mantle
They were synthesized only on early Earth
Delivered by meteorites, comets, and asteroids
They came from the Sun via solar wind
questions.explanation: Exogenous = from outside. The hypothesis says key organic molecules were made in space and delivered to early Earth by impacts.

questions.prompt: The Miller-Urey (endogenous) hypothesis suggests building blocks were made by:
choices: Volcanoes ejecting them from the mantle
questions.explanation: Miller-Urey ran sparks (lightning) through methane, ammonia, water, and hydrogen and got amino acids: showing organics could form on Earth itself.

questions.prompt: Describe the Heavy Bombardment Period and its potential significance for the origin of life on Earth.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Period ~4.1-3.8 Bya when early Earth and inner planets were heavily cratered by asteroids/comets. (2) Evidence visible on Moon and Mercury craters. (3) May have delivered water and organic building blocks (amino acids, nucleobases) via carbonaceous asteroids. (4) Could have both sterilized and re-seeded the young Earth.

questions.prompt: Explain how asteroids like Bennu support the hypothesis that building blocks of life were delivered to Earth.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) OSIRIS-REx returned samples from Bennu (2023). (2) Samples contained 14 of 20 amino acids life uses, plus 5 nucleobases used in DNA/RNA. (3) Ammonia + formaldehyde found together — precursors to amino acids. (4) Shows organic chemistry needed for life existed in early solar system, not unique to Earth.

questions.prompt: Name and briefly describe the three main asteroid/meteorite classification types.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Stony (chondrites + achondrites) — silicate rich, most common. (2) Iron — mostly iron-nickel, dense, from differentiated cores. (3) Stony-iron — mixture of silicate and metal (e.g., pallasites with olivine + iron). Carbonaceous chondrites are especially important for organics.

questions.prompt: Compare the roles of endogenous (Earth-made) vs exogenous (space-delivered) sources of life's building blocks.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Endogenous: Miller-Urey-style chemistry on Earth — lightning, heat, UV on atmospheric gases. (2) Exogenous: delivered by meteorites/comets carrying amino acids, nucleobases. (3) Most likely BOTH contributed. (4) Bennu evidence strongly supports exogenous contribution.

11.title: Origin of Life Part II: Building Blocks & First Life

questions.prompt: Which six elements are the core essentials for life on Earth (CHNOPS)?
choices: Calcium, hydrogen, neon, oxygen, potassium, silicon
Carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, sulfur
Carbon, helium, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, silicon
Cobalt, hydrogen, nickel, oxygen, phosphorus, sodium
questions.explanation: CHNOPS spells out the six: Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Phosphorus, Sulfur. They form proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, and carbohydrates.

questions.prompt: Carbon is central to biological molecules because:
choices: It is the most abundant element in the universe
It dissolves easily in salt water solutions
Four covalent bonds form chains and rings
It is naturally magnetic at room temperature
questions.explanation: Carbon's four valence electrons let it form long chains, rings, and complex 3D shapes, the structural backbone of every biomolecule.

questions.prompt: Which is NOT a characteristic of life (biological definition)?
questions.explanation: Reproduction, homeostasis, and energy processing are core traits of life. Magnetism is unrelated: many living things aren't magnetic and many non-living things are.

questions.prompt: Life on Earth uses which chirality?
questions.explanation: All Earth life uses L-amino acids and D-sugars/nucleic acids: a homochirality that is itself a clue about life's origins.

questions.prompt: Why is water considered essential for life?
choices: It conducts electricity perfectly when pure
It is naturally radioactive in nature
It is a fundamental chemical element
questions.explanation: Water's polarity makes it a universal solvent for ions and polar molecules; hydrogen bonding gives it high heat capacity, cohesion, and stable chemistry.

questions.prompt: Astronomers' three requirements for a habitable location are:
questions.explanation: Astrobiology's habitability triad: a solvent (liquid water), chemical nutrients (CHNOPS + others), and an energy source (sunlight, chemistry, or heat).

questions.prompt: The Miller-Urey experiment (1953) demonstrated that:
choices: The Earth is roughly billions of years old
Water boils at 100 C in laboratory air
Life only ever comes from prior life
Amino acids form abiotically with energy
questions.explanation: Sparks through methane, ammonia, water, and hydrogen produced amino acids: showing life's building blocks can arise from simple inorganic chemistry.

questions.prompt: Which is NOT one of the four mechanisms of evolution?
questions.explanation: The four mechanisms are mutation, natural selection, genetic drift, and gene flow. Magnetism doesn't change allele frequencies.

questions.prompt: Prokaryotes are characterized by:
choices: Having a nucleus and multiple cells
Having organelles but no actual DNA
Living only inside geothermal hot springs
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes (bacteria, archaea) are single-celled, lack a membrane-bound nucleus, and come in three main shapes: cocci, bacilli, spirilla.

questions.prompt: Assembly Theory (Sharma, Cronin, Walker 2023) attempts to:
choices: Prove that life is fundamentally impossible
Classify stars by their spectral signatures
questions.explanation: Assembly Theory measures how many steps are required to build a molecule. Above a complexity threshold, only selection (life) can plausibly explain it.

questions.prompt: List the six main elements essential for life (CHNOPS) and explain why carbon is central.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) C, H, N, O, P, S. (2) Carbon central because it has 4 valence electrons enabling 4 stable covalent bonds. (3) Forms chains and rings (hydrocarbons). (4) Functional groups attach and determine molecule behavior. (5) Versatility enables enormous biomolecule diversity.

questions.prompt: Why is liquid water considered essential for habitability? List at least 4 of its special properties.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Polar molecule, hydrogen bonds. (2) Excellent solvent for ions and polar molecules. (3) High heat capacity stabilizes temperature. (4) High heat of vaporization enables cooling. (5) Cohesion/adhesion, surface tension, capillary action. (6) Buffered pH for homeostasis.

questions.prompt: List the four mechanisms of evolution and briefly define each.
questions.rubric: Four mechanisms: (1) Natural selection — differential survival/reproduction based on heritable traits. (2) Mutation — random change in genetic code; source of new variation. (3) Genetic drift — random changes in allele frequencies, especially in small populations. (4) Gene flow — movement of alleles between populations via migration/interbreeding.

questions.prompt: Describe the Miller-Urey experiment and what it demonstrated.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) 1953 experiment simulating hypothesized early Earth atmosphere (water vapor, methane, ammonia, hydrogen). (2) Applied electrical sparks (lightning analog). (3) Produced amino acids abiotically. (4) Demonstrated that organic building blocks can form from inorganic precursors with energy input under primitive Earth-like conditions.

12.title: Limits of Life: Evolution and Extinction

questions.prompt: Why is the 'tree' (not ladder) analogy better for describing evolution?
choices: Because ladders are not found in nature
Evolution is branching, not linear progress
Because a tree visibly has leaves on it
Trees are prettier than ladders to depict
questions.explanation: Evolution branches: lineages split into many co-existing species. A ladder implies a linear march toward humans, which is biologically wrong.

questions.prompt: The background extinction rate is approximately:
questions.explanation: Roughly 1 species per million per year goes extinct under normal conditions. Mass extinctions blow past this baseline rate, sometimes by 1000x.

questions.prompt: How many major mass extinctions does the fossil record show?
questions.explanation: The 'Big Five' mass extinctions: Ordovician, Devonian, Permian (largest), Triassic, and Cretaceous-Paleogene (the dinosaur-killer).

questions.prompt: In the Drake Equation, L represents:
choices: The total luminosity of our sun
The total number of known galaxies
questions.explanation: L is how long a civilization broadcasts detectable signals. It dominates the answer because the others are reasonably constrained, but L spans centuries to billions of years.

questions.prompt: If all Drake Equation terms except L equal 1, the equation simplifies to:
questions.explanation: Multiplying anything by 1's leaves L. This shows why civilization lifetime is the most consequential term in Drake's equation.

questions.prompt: Extremophiles are:
choices: Star systems outside the habitable zone
Humans who travel to and explore space
Rare dinosaur species from the fossil record
questions.explanation: Extremophiles thrive in heat, cold, acid, salt, pressure, or radiation that would kill most life. They expand where we should look for life off Earth.

questions.prompt: Convergent evolution of intelligence (Roth 2015) suggests:
choices: Complex brains arose in multiple lineages
Only humans can become truly intelligent
Intelligence is impossible to evolve at all
questions.explanation: Mammals, birds, and cephalopods independently evolved complex cognition. Intelligence appears to be a repeatable evolutionary outcome, not a one-off.

questions.prompt: Why is the fossil record necessary for studying life's history?
choices: Only direct evidence of extinct organisms
It proves the Earth is geologically flat
Fossils are physically composed of DNA
questions.explanation: Fossils are our only window into species that no longer exist. Without them we'd have no record of dinosaurs, trilobites, or most of life's past.

questions.prompt: Explain extinction and the significance of mass extinctions in Earth's history.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Background extinction rate ~1 in 1 million species per year. (2) Fossil record shows 5 mass extinctions. (3) Mass extinctions involve much higher rates. (4) Current rates may be comparable (possible 6th extinction). (5) Mass extinctions reshape biodiversity and open niches for new radiations.

questions.prompt: What is 'L' in the Drake Equation and why is it the most uncertain term?
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) L = lifetime (years) of a civilization releasing detectable signals. (2) Earth has had radio for ~100 years. (3) Could be centuries (if civ destroys itself) to billions (if stable). (4) Massive uncertainty (1,000 to 5 billion). (5) If all other Drake terms equal 1, N = L.

questions.prompt: Describe extremophiles and give at least 3 environmental extremes they can tolerate. Why are they relevant to astrobiology?
questions.rubric: Key points: Extremophiles survive/thrive in extreme environments. At least 3 of: (1) Temperature — thermophiles (hot), psychrophiles (cold). (2) pH — acidophiles, alkaliphiles. (3) Pressure — piezophiles/barophiles. (4) Salinity — halophiles. (5) Radiation — radioresistant organisms. Relevance: broadens habitable zone for astrobiology.

questions.prompt: Newton's THIRD law applied to rockets says:
choices: Rockets don't work in a vacuum at all
Heavier rockets go faster than light ones
Fuel always burns at a constant rate
questions.explanation: Action = reaction: hot gas expelled downward produces equal and opposite upward thrust. Mass and burn rate matter for acceleration but the third law itself is about the force pair.

questions.prompt: Mass vs weight: a rocket on the Moon has:
choices: Less mass but the same Earth weight
Half its Earth mass and weight
questions.explanation: Mass is a property of the object and stays fixed. Weight is mass times local gravity, so on the Moon (g ~ 1.6 m/s^2) the weight drops to ~1/6.

questions.prompt: Mars gravity is approximately what fraction of Earth's?
questions.explanation: Mars surface gravity is roughly 3.7 m/s^2, about 38% (~1/3) of Earth's 9.8 m/s^2. Useful when planning landers and surface ops.

questions.prompt: Weight on a rocket acts through which point?
questions.explanation: Gravity acts at the center of gravity. The center of pressure is where aerodynamic force acts; offsets between the two cause torque and rotation.

questions.prompt: Which type of drag is caused by the rocket's shape and pressure differences (including base drag)?
questions.explanation: Form (pressure) drag depends on shape and the low-pressure wake behind the rocket. Skin friction is surface roughness; wave drag is shock-related at high speed.

questions.prompt: Rocket staging is used because:
choices: It simply looks cool during launch
It saves fuel storage costs on Earth
Each stage operates on different physics
questions.explanation: Once a fuel tank is empty it's just dead weight. Jettisoning it reduces mass so later stages get more acceleration from the same thrust.

questions.prompt: Falcon 9 launch cost (~$2,600/kg) is notable because:
choices: It only carries small science satellites
About half of comparable traditional rockets
It does not actually reach orbit at all
It is the most expensive rocket built
questions.explanation: Reusability and in-house manufacturing drove Falcon 9's cost to roughly half of expendable competitors, reshaping the launch market.

questions.prompt: Starship's goal for reusability is to reduce cost per kg to orbit to:
questions.explanation: Full reusability of both stages plus rapid turnaround targets sub-$100/kg, an order-of-magnitude drop from Falcon 9 and the threshold for true space industrialization.

questions.prompt: Earth's escape velocity is approximately:
questions.explanation: Earth's escape velocity is ~11.2 km/s. Below that you fall back; above that you can leave Earth's gravity well entirely.

questions.prompt: Rocket engines can gimbal (tilt) to:
choices: Create torque to steer the rocket
questions.explanation: Tilting the thrust vector off the rocket's center of gravity creates a torque, which rotates the vehicle. This is the main way modern rockets steer.

questions.prompt: State Newton's three laws of motion and explain how each applies to a rocket launch.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) First law (inertia) — rocket at rest stays unless force applied; drag/gravity must be overcome. (2) Second law F=ma — thrust must exceed weight to accelerate; as fuel burns, mass decreases so acceleration increases. (3) Third law — action/reaction: expelling hot gas downward produces upward thrust. All three essential.

questions.prompt: What forces act on a rocket in flight? Name at least 4 and describe each.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Thrust — from engine, along long axis, can gimbal. (2) Weight — gravity pulling toward Earth center, acts at center of gravity. (3) Lift — aerodynamic, sideways, stabilizing, generated by nose/body/fins. (4) Drag — skin friction + form drag, opposes motion. (5) Torque — rotational effect when forces don't pass through center of gravity.

questions.prompt: How has SpaceX's business model affected its ability to innovate rocket technology? Use Falcon 9/Heavy/Starship cost data.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Self-funded (not traditional government contracts) enables faster iteration and risk tolerance. (2) Falcon 9 ~ $2,600/kg — roughly half comparable rockets. (3) Falcon Heavy ~ $1,500/kg. (4) Starship (reusable both stages) aims under $100/kg. (5) MAHD / rapid iteration culture. (6) Reusability = primary cost driver.

questions.prompt: The 'working fluid' of a rocket refers to:
choices: The water cooling system on the engine
Hot exhaust gas expelled by the nozzle
Hydraulic fluid in the steering system
questions.explanation: The working fluid is what gets accelerated and ejected to create thrust: hot combustion gas in chemical rockets, or compressed air in stomp rockets.

questions.prompt: Aerodynamic forces can cause a rocket to rotate because:
choices: Gravity always pulls sideways on flight
Center of pressure differs from gravity
The engines always misfire during ascent
questions.explanation: When the center of pressure (where aero force acts) is offset from the center of gravity, that offset creates torque, which rotates the rocket.

questions.prompt: Drag on a rocket is GREATEST when:
choices: Engine is off; rocket coasts with base drag
When the rocket is fully in a vacuum
When the velocity is exactly zero m/s
The engine is firing at full thrust
questions.explanation: With the engine off, the low-pressure wake behind the rocket isn't filled by exhaust, so base drag spikes. Vacuum and zero velocity have no drag at all.

questions.prompt: Skin friction drag depends on:
choices: The combined weight of the crew
Only the total mass of the rocket
questions.explanation: Skin friction comes from air shearing along the rocket's surface: smoother skin, lower viscosity, and the right Reynolds number all reduce it.

questions.prompt: Rocket motion is best described as having two coupled components:
choices: Translation from forces and rotation from torques
Only thrust and drag working in combination
Mass and weight as separate entities
questions.explanation: Net forces accelerate the rocket through space (translation); net torques rotate it about its center of gravity. Both must be controlled.

questions.prompt: A compressed-air or stomp rocket produces thrust the same way a chemical rocket does because:
choices: Both use solid fuel during ignition
Both expel working fluid via Newton's 3rd
Both rely on chemical fuel combustion
Both require an oxidizer to ignite
questions.explanation: Both push working fluid out one end and get pushed the other way via Newton's third law. The energy source differs (compression vs combustion) but the principle is identical.

questions.prompt: Falcon 9 v1.0 to v1.1 changed primarily:
choices: The destination from orbit to Mars surface
The fuel changed from kerosene to solid
questions.explanation: v1.1 reorganized the nine Merlin engines into the octaweb pattern and added landing legs/grid fins, the foundation for first-stage reusability.

questions.prompt: Grasshopper was SpaceX's:
questions.explanation: Grasshopper was a low-altitude VTOL testbed (2012-2013) that proved a Falcon-class rocket could land vertically: the foundation of Falcon 9 reusability.

questions.prompt: MAHD (the development process associated with SpaceX) differs from traditional aerospace engineering by:
questions.explanation: MAHD (Modern Agile Hardware Development) builds, breaks, and iterates fast. Traditional aerospace uses waterfall: years of paper design before any hardware.

questions.prompt: The Planetary Exploration Budget Dataset (Planetary Society) is most useful for:
choices: Tracking the health of individual rovers
Comparing mission and launch service costs
questions.explanation: The dataset lets you compare actual mission cost and launch service prices across decades of NASA missions, useful for trade studies.

questions.prompt: Why is full-scale rocket aerodynamic drag often LESS critical than for model rockets?
choices: Full-scale rockets are far slower in flight
Full-scale rockets carry no fins at all
There is essentially no difference at all
Less time spent in dense atmosphere
questions.explanation: Full-scale rockets accelerate quickly through the dense lower atmosphere, then spend most of the flight in thin air or vacuum. Model rockets spend their whole flight in dense air.

questions.prompt: Engineers track rocket performance using mass ratios because:
choices: Mass ratios determine paint coat thickness
Mass ratios apply only to small satellites
Mass ratio measures rocket paint color
questions.explanation: The mass ratio (initial/final mass) plugs directly into the rocket equation: more propellant per unit dry mass means more achievable delta-v.

questions.prompt: You are designing a launch system for a 5,000 kg payload to Mars. (a) Identify the four major forces acting on the rocket during ascent and explain which is hardest to overcome at launch. (b) Compare a SpaceX Falcon Heavy launch (~$1,500/kg) to a traditional rocket — name two innovations that drive the cost difference. (c) How would the launch dynamics change if you launched the same rocket from the lunar surface instead?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Thrust, weight, lift, drag. Weight is hardest at launch — the rocket is heaviest (full fuel) and gravity is strongest near the surface. (b) Innovations: reusable first stage (Grasshopper-derived), engine arrangement / Merlin engine cluster, vertical takeoff and landing, propellant cross-feeding, in-house manufacturing, MAHD-style rapid iteration. (c) Lunar surface: 1/6 Earth gravity → much less thrust required; no atmosphere → no drag, no aerodynamic stability needed (no fins, no nose-cone optimization), but no oxygen for air-breathing engines either; escape velocity is ~2.4 km/s vs 11.2 km/s on Earth.

questions.prompt: Compare MAHD to traditional aerospace engineering, then evaluate when MAHD is the right approach. (a) What is one product MAHD would be a good fit for, and why? (b) What is one product where MAHD would be a poor fit, and why? (c) How does MAHD align with Babson's ET&A framework?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Good fit: products with rapid feedback loops, modular hardware, low cost-per-iteration (e.g., consumer electronics, software, certain reusable launch vehicles where scrap-and-rebuild is feasible). (b) Poor fit: products requiring extreme upfront certification or where failure is catastrophic and irreversible (e.g., nuclear reactors, single-shot interplanetary probes, certified medical devices). (c) ET&A: entrepreneurial leadership through risk management, continuous learning, deep collaboration — MAHD operationalizes these by tolerating failure, learning fast, and integrating disciplines, mirroring the entrepreneurial mindset Babson teaches.

questions.prompt: Remote sensing instruments:
choices: Measure objects at a distance via light
Make physical contact with the surface
Only work on Earth, not in deep space
questions.explanation: Remote sensing collects light (or other EM radiation) from afar: cameras, telescopes, spectrometers. No contact with the target needed.

questions.prompt: Direct sensing (contact science) instruments:
choices: Only measure surface or ambient temperature
Require a dedicated human operator nearby
questions.explanation: Direct sensing measures whatever is physically near the instrument: surface composition under a rover, gases in a probe's intake, plasma at the spacecraft's location.

questions.prompt: Sojourner's APXS (alpha-particle X-ray spectrometer) is an example of:
questions.explanation: APXS pressed against rocks and bombarded them with alpha particles to read composition: a textbook contact-science instrument.

questions.prompt: Voyager I and II carried how many science investigations?
questions.explanation: Each Voyager carried 11 distinct science investigations spanning imaging, spectroscopy, particles, fields, and plasma waves.

questions.prompt: Which Artemis-era payload measures lunar subsurface heat flow by drilling?
questions.explanation: LISTER (Lunar Instrumentation for Subsurface Thermal Exploration with Rapidity) drills into the regolith to measure heat flow from the Moon's interior.

questions.prompt: SELINE's purpose is to:
choices: Measure cosmic rays and regolith effects
Launch new missions from lunar surface
Drill for water in the lunar regolith
Image the lunar surface in 3D thermal
questions.explanation: SELINE measures cosmic ray flux and how it interacts with lunar regolith, important for protecting future astronauts.

questions.prompt: Compare remote sensing and direct sensing instruments. Give an example of each.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Remote sensing — measures from a distance by gathering/focusing light; examples: cameras, telescopes, Cassini optical remote sensing. (2) Direct sensing / contact science — measures phenomena in immediate vicinity; examples: Sojourner's APXS (alpha-particle X-ray spectrometer), mass spectrometers. (3) Missions typically use both to maximize science return.

questions.prompt: Pick any location in the Solar System (e.g., Titan, Europa, Enceladus) and describe what payload instruments you would include to search for life there. Justify each choice.
questions.rubric: Good answers should: (1) Name the target and its conditions (e.g., Europa's subsurface ocean, Titan's methane lakes). (2) List 3-5 instruments with rationale: imagers for surface/plumes, spectrometers for composition, radar for subsurface, mass spectrometers for organics, magnetometer for ocean confirmation, seismometers, thermal imagers. (3) Tie each instrument to habitability/biosignature detection.

questions.prompt: When designing a mission, how do scientific goals drive payload selection, and how do cost/mass constraints affect what you can include?
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Goals come first — 'what question are we answering?'. (2) Instruments chosen to measure relevant signals. (3) Every instrument has mass, power, data budget. (4) Rocket payload capacity limits total mass. (5) Trade-offs: more instruments = heavier/more expensive rocket. (6) Justification required for each inclusion.

15.title: Searching for Life in the Solar System

questions.prompt: Titan's atmosphere is approximately:
questions.explanation: Titan's atmosphere is mostly N2 (~95%) with ~5% methane and a stew of carbon-rich organics, the only thick atmosphere on a moon in our solar system.

questions.prompt: Titan's atmospheric density compared to Earth's:
questions.explanation: Titan's surface pressure is ~1.5 atm and the atmosphere extends much higher than Earth's; total column density is roughly 4x Earth's.

questions.prompt: Titan's surface features liquid:
choices: Liquid iron flowing across the surface
Vast water oceans on the surface
Methane and ethane lakes and rivers
questions.explanation: At ~94 K, methane and ethane stay liquid. Titan has a full hydrocarbon hydrologic cycle: rain, rivers, and polar lakes/seas.

questions.prompt: The Huygens probe (2005):
choices: Left the solar system after Saturn flyby
Landed on Mars and roved the surface
Parachuted to Titan and imaged terrain
Orbited the planet Mercury for years
questions.explanation: Huygens, deployed by Cassini, parachuted through Titan's atmosphere and returned the only surface images we have of this moon.

questions.prompt: Enceladus is notable for:
choices: Being the largest body in the solar system
Tiger-stripe plumes of vapor, salts, organics
Lacking any detectable water on or in it
Its bright, deeply red surface coloration
questions.explanation: Enceladus's south-pole 'tiger stripes' jet plumes of water vapor, salts, and organics into space, sampling its subsurface ocean for free.

questions.prompt: Evidence for Enceladus's subsurface ocean includes:
choices: The moon's rotation rate around Saturn
Gravity wobble plus E-ring silica grains
Only visible-light photographs of the surface
questions.explanation: Cassini's gravity/libration data implies a global liquid layer; silica nanograins in Saturn's E ring point to hot hydrothermal water-rock chemistry inside.

questions.prompt: Europa is a moon of which planet?
questions.explanation: Europa is one of Jupiter's four large Galilean moons. Its icy shell hides a global subsurface ocean.

questions.prompt: Galileo was intentionally crashed into Jupiter at end of mission to:
questions.explanation: Planetary protection: NASA disposed of Galileo in Jupiter to ensure it could never accidentally impact Europa and contaminate its ocean with Earth microbes.

questions.prompt: Which Europa Clipper instrument uses radar to probe ice thickness and detect the ocean?
questions.explanation: REASON (Radar for Europa Assessment and Sounding: Ocean to Near-surface) penetrates the ice with radio waves to map the ice shell and ocean interface.

questions.prompt: Europa Clipper's MASPEX is a:
questions.explanation: MASPEX measures the masses of gas molecules in Europa's thin exosphere or any plumes, identifying organics and other ocean chemistry.

questions.prompt: Why is Titan considered a promising target for astrobiology? Describe its composition and surface features.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Largest moon of Saturn. (2) Atmosphere ~95% nitrogen, ~5% methane, plus carbon-rich compounds (prebiotic chemistry). (3) Atmosphere ~4x denser than Earth's; gravity ~1/7 Earth. (4) Has rivers, lakes, seas of liquid methane/ethane. (5) Sand dunes, vast plains. (6) Methane acts like water on Earth. (7) Complex organics abundant.

questions.prompt: Why is Enceladus a compelling target for finding life? Describe the evidence for its subsurface ocean and activity.
questions.rubric: Key points: (1) Icy moon of Saturn, most reflective body in solar system. (2) Tiger stripes — warm fractures near south pole releasing plumes of water vapor, CO2, methane, ammonia, salts, organics. (3) Organic density 20x higher than expected. (4) Gravity/wobble measurements show liquid water beneath ice. (5) Silica nanograins in E ring suggest hydrothermal vents at ~90C. (6) Plumes sampled by Cassini — proxy for ocean composition without drilling.

questions.prompt: Compare Titan, Europa, and Enceladus as targets for finding life. Which would you prioritize and why?
questions.rubric: Strong answers should: (1) Compare habitability factors. (2) Discuss accessibility — Enceladus plumes are sample-ready without drilling; Europa requires ice penetration; Titan requires entry/landing. (3) Justify prioritization with tradeoffs between science return and mission cost/risk.

16.title: Searching for Life Pt 2: Mission Deep Dive

questions.prompt: Voyager (1979-1980) was unable to image Titan's surface because:
choices: Titan has no actual rocky surface itself
Thick organic haze blocked visible light
The onboard cameras malfunctioned at Saturn
Voyager never reached the Saturn system
questions.explanation: Titan's nitrogen-methane atmosphere produces a thick orange organic smog that scatters and absorbs visible light. Voyager's cameras saw only the haze.

questions.prompt: In 1994, Hubble Space Telescope made progress observing Titan by using:
questions.explanation: Near-infrared light penetrates Titan's haze better than visible. Hubble's NIR observations revealed the first hints of surface features beneath the smog.

questions.prompt: Cassini classified Titan as an 'ocean world' after finding:
choices: Evidence of a salty subsurface ocean
Visible liquid water oceans on surface
Methane and ethane surface lakes only
questions.explanation: Cassini's gravity and rotation measurements revealed a salty liquid water ocean beneath Titan's icy crust, distinct from the methane lakes on the surface.

questions.prompt: On Titan, raindrops fall slowly because:
choices: Titan has no rain at all
questions.explanation: Titan's gravity is ~1.35 m/s^2 and the atmosphere is dense, so methane raindrops fall lazily, almost in slow motion compared to Earth rain.

questions.prompt: Dragonfly is a planned NASA mission to:
questions.explanation: Dragonfly is a nuclear-powered rotorcraft launching mid-decade to fly between sites on Titan, sampling its organic-rich surface and atmosphere.

questions.prompt: Cassini measured plume speeds from Enceladus's tiger stripes at approximately:
questions.explanation: Plume material exits the tiger stripes at roughly 400 m/s (~800 mph), fast enough to escape Enceladus's tiny gravity and feed Saturn's E ring.

questions.prompt: Silica nanograins detected in Saturn's E ring suggest:
choices: Pure water ice with no chemistry
Active volcanic activity on Saturn itself
questions.explanation: Silica nanograins form when warm (~90 C) alkaline water reacts with rock, the signature of hydrothermal activity on Enceladus's seafloor.

questions.prompt: The Orbilander mission concept is designed to:
choices: Orbit and survey the planet Mars
Visit dwarf planet Pluto and Charon
questions.explanation: Orbilander first orbits Enceladus to fly through plumes, then lands at the south pole to sample fresh material directly from the tiger stripes.

questions.prompt: Europa's average distance from the Sun is approximately:
questions.explanation: Europa orbits Jupiter, which sits ~5.2 AU from the Sun. That distance keeps the surface frozen but tidal flexing keeps the interior warm.

questions.prompt: Europa's average surface temperature is approximately:
questions.explanation: Europa's icy surface sits at about -160°C. The subsurface ocean stays liquid because of tidal heating from Jupiter.

questions.prompt: Galileo (1995-2003) was intentionally sent into Jupiter at end-of-mission to:
choices: Take a final photograph of Jupiter
questions.explanation: Planetary protection: NASA disposed of Galileo in Jupiter to ensure it could never accidentally crash into Europa and contaminate its ocean.

questions.prompt: Juno's September 2022 close flyby of Europa came within approximately:
questions.explanation: Juno passed within ~352 km of Europa in September 2022, the closest flyby since Galileo and a preview of what Clipper will see up close.

questions.prompt: JUICE (Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer), launched April 2023 by ESA, will study:
questions.explanation: JUICE targets the three icy Galilean moons, ending in orbit around Ganymede, the largest moon in the solar system and itself an ocean world.

questions.prompt: The Europa Clipper instrument that maps ices, salts, organics, and heat patterns in infrared is:
questions.explanation: MISE (Mapping Imaging Spectrometer for Europa) splits IR light to identify surface ices, salts, and organic compounds.

questions.prompt: Europa-UVS detects atmospheric gases, surface materials, and possible plumes using:
questions.explanation: Europa-UVS observes UV emission and absorption to fingerprint atmospheric gases and look for plumes against starlight or Jupiter's glow.

questions.prompt: ECM on Europa Clipper is a:
questions.explanation: ECM (Europa Clipper Magnetometer) measures induced magnetic fields, confirming a salty conducting ocean and constraining ice shell thickness.

questions.prompt: PIMS on Europa Clipper analyzes plasma to:
questions.explanation: PIMS measures Jupiter's plasma environment so the magnetometer can subtract it and isolate the magnetic signal coming from Europa's ocean.

questions.prompt: SUDA on Europa Clipper examines:
questions.explanation: SUDA (Surface Dust Analyzer) catches and identifies micron-sized grains kicked off Europa's surface by impacts, sampling the surface without landing.

questions.prompt: The Europa Imaging System (EIS) consists of:
questions.explanation: EIS pairs a wide-angle and narrow-angle camera for global mapping plus high-resolution geology, giving context and detail in the same dataset.

questions.prompt: E-THEMIS distinguishes warmer regions on Europa where liquid water may be near the surface using:
questions.explanation: E-THEMIS senses thermal IR to find heat anomalies. Recent surface activity or thin ice would show up as hotter spots.

questions.prompt: Compare Cassini's discoveries at Titan and Enceladus. (a) Name one piece of evidence from each moon that supports a subsurface ocean. (b) Why was Cassini's instrument suite able to make BOTH discoveries on a single mission? (c) Which target would you prioritize for follow-up, and which proposed mission concept (Dragonfly, Orbilander, Europa Clipper) would you use? Justify.
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Titan: gravity/wobble measurements show salty subsurface ocean; surface methane/ethane lakes plus radar mapping showed an icy crust. Enceladus: tiger-stripe plumes containing water vapor, salts, organics; gravity/wobble measurements; silica nanograins in E ring suggesting hydrothermal vents. (b) Cassini carried a multi-instrument suite (radar, IR/UV imaging, mass spectrometers, magnetometers, plasma instruments) and could fly close enough to both moons to sample plumes and map terrain across many flybys. (c) Strong answers prioritize Enceladus (plumes are sample-ready, no drilling needed) with Orbilander, OR Titan with Dragonfly (heavy organics, complex chemistry), justified by access difficulty and science return.

questions.prompt: Europa Clipper carries a complementary suite of instruments. (a) Pick three instruments from the Clipper payload and explain what each measures. (b) Why does NASA need this combination instead of one super-instrument? (c) How would the combined data from your three instruments be used to assess habitability?
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Three from: EIS (imagers — geology, elevation), E-THEMIS (IR thermal, near-surface liquid water signs), Europa-UVS (UV — gases, plumes), MISE (IR mapping of ices/salts/organics), ECM (magnetometer — confirm ocean and ice shell), PIMS (plasma — disentangle magnetic signal), REASON (radar — ice thickness, ocean), MASPEX (mass spectrometer — gases and plume composition), SUDA (dust — surface composition, ocean salinity), Gravity/Radio Science (interior structure, tidal flexing). (b) Each instrument addresses a different physical signal; habitability is multi-dimensional (chemistry, energy, water, time) so cross-validation across instruments reduces ambiguity and adds redundancy. (c) Tie measurements together: e.g., REASON confirms ocean → ECM confirms it's salty/conductive → MASPEX/SUDA characterize chemistry → MISE identifies organics → habitability case becomes coherent.

questions.prompt: Planetary protection is a key constraint for missions to potentially habitable worlds. (a) Describe what Galileo did at end-of-mission and why. (b) How does this constrain design decisions for future Europa or Enceladus missions? (c) Discuss the tradeoff between maximizing scientific access (e.g., landing) and minimizing contamination risk.
questions.rubric: Key points: (a) Galileo was intentionally crashed into Jupiter at end-of-mission to prevent any chance the spacecraft would later impact Europa and contaminate its subsurface ocean with Earth microbes. (b) Future missions must: sterilize hardware, plan disposal trajectories, limit landing operations, follow COSPAR planetary protection categories, and budget for contamination control. (c) Tradeoff: landing/sampling enables direct biosignature detection but raises contamination risk and cost; flybys (Europa Clipper) and plume sampling (Orbilander) reduce contamination risk but limit ground-truth. Strong answers acknowledge that 'discovering' life with a contaminated probe would be scientifically worthless, so protection is not optional.

18.title: Interstellar Travel: Distances, Standard Candles & Relativity

questions.prompt: The cosmic distance ladder is best described as:
choices: A single technique used at every distance scale
Overlapping techniques calibrated rung by rung
An old theory now replaced by Hubble's Law
A literal ladder used to mount telescopes
questions.explanation: No one method works at every scale. Each rung (parallax, Cepheids, supernovae, Hubble's Law) is calibrated using the rung below it.

questions.prompt: Radar ranging is primarily used to measure distances:
choices: To distant galaxies beyond our own
Out to the edge of the observable universe
Within the solar system (~1 billion km)
To Cepheid variable stars in nearby galaxies
questions.explanation: Radar bounces off planets and asteroids and times the round trip. It only works inside the solar system; signals get too weak much farther.

questions.prompt: One Astronomical Unit (AU) is defined as:
choices: The radius of the Milky Way galaxy
The distance from Earth to the Moon
questions.explanation: 1 AU = ~150 million km, the Earth-Sun distance. It's the natural ruler for solar-system distances.

questions.prompt: A light-year is approximately:
choices: Roughly equal to one parsec of distance
Roughly equal to one Astronomical Unit
The distance the Sun travels in one year
questions.explanation: A light-year is how far light travels in one year: ~9.46 trillion km. Different from a parsec (~3.26 ly) and vastly bigger than an AU.

questions.prompt: A parsec is approximately:
questions.explanation: A parsec is the distance at which 1 AU subtends 1 arcsecond, ~3.26 light-years. It's the natural unit for parallax-based distances.

questions.prompt: If a star has a parallax angle P (in arcseconds), its distance D in parsecs is:
questions.explanation: Distance in parsecs equals the reciprocal of parallax in arcseconds. A star with 0.5 arcsec parallax is 2 pc away.

questions.prompt: Stellar parallax becomes unusable beyond roughly:
choices: The edge of the observable universe
questions.explanation: At greater distances the parallax angle shrinks below measurement precision. Gaia extends the limit but it still tops out a few hundred parsecs reliably.

questions.prompt: The distance modulus equation relates:
choices: Apparent and absolute magnitude with d
questions.explanation: m - M = 5 log(d) - 5 lets you solve for distance d once you know how bright a star appears (m) and how bright it really is (M).

questions.prompt: Apparent magnitude (m) measures:
choices: How bright a star appears from Earth
The intrinsic brightness of a star
questions.explanation: Apparent magnitude is observed brightness from Earth. Absolute magnitude is what the star would look like at a standard 10 pc distance.

questions.prompt: A 'standard candle' is:
choices: A reference scale for stellar mass
An object with known intrinsic luminosity
questions.explanation: If you know the true brightness, you can compare it to apparent brightness and solve for distance. Cepheids and Type Ia supernovae are the workhorses.

questions.prompt: Cepheid variable stars are useful as standard candles because:
choices: They all share an identical luminosity
They never change brightness over time at all
They are the brightest stars in every galaxy
questions.explanation: The longer a Cepheid's pulse period, the brighter it intrinsically is. Measure the period, get absolute magnitude, then solve for distance.

questions.prompt: Henrietta Leavitt discovered the Cepheid Period-Luminosity relationship by studying variable stars in:
questions.explanation: All LMC Cepheids are at roughly the same distance, so apparent brightness differences directly reflected luminosity differences. That cracked the P-L relation.

questions.prompt: The Tully-Fisher relation links:
choices: A star's mass to its main-sequence lifetime
A planet's orbital path to its size
A galaxy's color to its overall age
questions.explanation: Faster-rotating spiral galaxies are intrinsically brighter. Measure the rotation curve, get luminosity, then derive distance, a galactic-scale standard candle.

questions.prompt: Type Ia supernovae work as standard candles because:
choices: They emit light only in the X-ray band
Detonation at Chandrasekhar mass; same peak
They occur at the same rate in every galaxy
They are the brightest objects in the universe
questions.explanation: Type Ia supernovae explode when a white dwarf hits the Chandrasekhar mass (~1.4 solar masses), so peak brightness is consistent and visible across the universe.

questions.prompt: The Chandrasekhar limit is approximately:
questions.explanation: Above ~1.4 solar masses, electron degeneracy pressure can't support a white dwarf and it collapses or detonates. This sharp limit is why Type Ia peak brightness is consistent.

questions.prompt: Hubble's Law states that:
choices: Light always travels at constant speed
The whole universe is contracting now
questions.explanation: v = H0 x d. Galaxies recede faster the farther they are: evidence the universe is expanding.

questions.prompt: Proxima Centauri is about 4.25 light-years away. At ~450,000 mph (Parker Solar Probe top speed), travel time would be roughly:
questions.explanation: Even at the fastest human-built speed ever, crossing 4.25 ly takes roughly 6,600 years. Interstellar distances dwarf solar-system distances.

questions.prompt: Special Relativity primarily deals with:
choices: Inertial frames and constant speed of light
questions.explanation: Special Relativity (1905) covers physics in inertial (non-accelerating) frames, with the speed of light constant for all observers. General Relativity adds gravity and curved spacetime.

questions.prompt: General Relativity describes gravity as:
choices: Spacetime curvature from mass and energy
A force transmitted by mediating particles
A property only of large planets and stars
An optical illusion at every physical scale
questions.explanation: In GR, mass and energy curve spacetime; objects follow the straightest available paths through that curvature, which we feel as gravity.

questions.prompt: Time dilation predicts that:
choices: Time runs slower at relativistic speed
Time stops entirely at very low velocities
Time runs faster as you approach light speed
Time is identical for all observers always
questions.explanation: Clocks moving relative to you tick slower. The effect is tiny at everyday speeds but becomes dramatic as v approaches c.

questions.prompt: The Lorentz factor (gamma) becomes large when:
choices: An object is fully at rest
questions.explanation: Gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - v^2/c^2). At rest gamma=1; as v approaches c, gamma blows up toward infinity. That's what drives extreme time dilation and length contraction.

questions.prompt: The Twin Paradox describes:
choices: A traveling twin returns younger than home twin
The doubling of mass at exactly the speed of light
Why two twins must always remain the same age
Two binary-system stars aging at the same rate
questions.explanation: The traveling twin's clock runs slow (and they accelerate, breaking symmetry between frames), so they return younger than the Earthbound twin.

questions.prompt: Length contraction states that:
